title: Test Coil Shape and Hall Effect Elements
narrationScript: This module has covered what the part does to coil impedance, and what frequency, coupling, and field strength do. One system factor remains — the coil itself. Geometry sets the empty coil's inductance: double the turns and inductance quadruples, and the operating point on the impedance plane moves with it. Field footprint follows diameter. Under a surface coil, eddy currents flow in a ring — essentially zero on the axis, strongest beneath the windings — so a small coil makes a small crack disturb a large fraction of its field, while a large coil averages over volume, which is exactly what conductivity sorting wants. Winding length, ferrite cores, and shields tune resolution further. And remember that eddy currents mirror the winding: an encircling coil's circumferential currents catch axial flaws and can run straight past circumferential ones. Finally, the Hall element reads flux density itself — flat from DC to about one hundred kilohertz — where a coil's output collapses as frequency falls. Match coil shape and detector to the requirement, and the signal follows.

sections.heading: The Coil Is a Test Parameter, Not a Window
sections.body:
This module began with the part — conductivity, permeability, mass, homogeneity — then moved to system settings: frequency, coupling, field strength. One system factor remains, and it is the one most often treated as furniture: the **test coil itself**. Its size and shape are selections you make, and they set the impedance baseline that everything else moves around.

Start with the empty coil. Its impedance combines winding resistance R and inductive reactance **X₀ = 2πfL₀**, and geometry writes both:

- **Turns.** Inductance scales with the **square of the number of turns** — L₀ ∝ N². Double the turns and L₀ quadruples.
- **Area and length.** For a solenoid-style winding, L₀ grows with the winding's cross-sectional area and shrinks as the same turns stretch over a longer winding (L = μN²A/l in the long-coil case).
- **Core.** A ferrite core gathers more flux through the same copper, raising L₀ again.
- **Wire gauge.** Finer wire packs more turns but adds resistance; the ratio ωL/R — the coil's Q — governs how cleanly small impedance changes resolve on the display.

Why a Level II cares: the normalized impedance plane you interpret is referenced to X₀, so geometry literally positions the operating point. And the winding's distributed capacitance, together with the cable's, forms a resonant circuit with L₀ — the probe is usable only **below its self-resonant frequency**. That is why probes carry a marked frequency range, and why a long extension cable can drag a probe's resonance down toward your test frequency.

sections.heading: Field Footprint: What the Coil Actually Illuminates
sections.body:
Set a circular surface coil on a part and the induced eddy currents **mirror the winding**: they flow in closed circles directly beneath the conductors. The density map matters:

- **Zero on the axis.** At the exact center of the coil, the circulating currents cancel — eddy current density is essentially zero on the coil's centerline.
- **Peak beneath the windings.** Density is greatest in a ring roughly under the mean winding radius, then tapers off outside the coil.

Two working consequences. First, a tiny flaw sitting dead-center under a pencil probe answers weakly; it speaks loudest as the winding ring passes over it — scan plans owe their **index and overlap requirements** to this ring-shaped sensitivity. Second, the footprint does not stop at the copper: the field of an **unshielded** coil spreads laterally beyond the winding, which is why edge effect announces a part edge before the probe physically reaches it.

Depth follows diameter too. The axial field of a surface coil decays within a distance on the order of the **coil radius** — a separate ceiling from the skin-depth limit δ = 1/√(πfμσ). Drop the frequency as far as you like: a 3 mm pencil probe still cannot interrogate 10 mm down, because its field never gets there. Deep-penetration probes are therefore **physically large** as well as low-frequency, and whichever ceiling is lower — coil size or skin depth — wins.

sections.caption: Eddy current density under a surface coil: zero on the axis, peaking beneath the windings — a small coil concentrates its field for resolution, a large coil spreads and averages it.

sections.title: Signal amplitude follows the fraction of the field disturbed
sections.body: The impedance change a discontinuity produces is governed by **how large a share of the total eddy current flow it interrupts**. A 1 mm fatigue crack under a 3 mm shielded probe blocks a meaningful fraction of a small, concentrated field — strong signal. The same crack under a 25 mm sorting coil disturbs a tiny share of a large averaged field and disappears into the baseline. Match the footprint to the question: coil diameter **comparable to the flaw** when you must detect it; coil diameter **deliberately oversized** when you want bulk properties — conductivity, heat treat — with surface noise averaged away.

sections.heading: Shape Decisions: Length, Cores, Shields, and Orientation
sections.body:
Each geometry feature is a lever with a known direction:

- **Winding length (bobbin and encircling coils).** A short coil concentrates its field over a short axial zone — it resolves short pits and cracks, and a short **differential pair** cancels gradual variation. A long coil averages along its length: short defects smear out, but slow trends — gradual wall thinning, dimension or conductivity drift — read cleanly, especially in an **absolute** connection.
- **Ferrite cores.** A high-permeability, low-conductivity core concentrates flux through the same winding: more inductance and sensitivity from a smaller package, which is how sub-3 mm probes stay usable.
- **Shields and cup cores.** A ferrite shield ring or cup confines the field laterally, pulling the footprint back to roughly the winding diameter. That is the probe you bring to **edges, radii, and fastener heads** — the geometry signal that would bury a crack indication stays outside the shield.
- **Driver–pickup (reflection) windings.** Separating excitation from sensing lets you size each for its own job — a large driver to establish the field, a small pickup or a Hall element to read it — and broadens the usable bandwidth.

And one orientation rule worth tattooing on the clipboard: **eddy currents mirror the winding, and a flaw must interrupt them to be seen.** An encircling coil drives currents around the tube's circumference, so it finds **axial** seams and cracks well — and can run straight past a thin **circumferential** crack lying parallel to the current flow. ASTM E243 carries that caveat for exactly this reason.

sections.caption: Four geometry levers: a ferrite shield confines the footprint for work beside fasteners; an unshielded pancake spills field sideways; an encircling coil's circumferential currents cut across axial flaws but run parallel to circumferential ones; bobbin length trades resolution for averaging.
Matching geometry and detector to the impedance-response requirement.
rows: Crack detection at fastener heads and edges
Small shielded, ferrite-cored surface probe
Confined footprint keeps geometry signals out; a small field makes a small crack a large disturbance
Conductivity sorting and heat-treat verification
Large-diameter air-core absolute probe
Broad footprint averages roughness and microstructure into a stable bulk reading
Short pits in heat-exchanger tubing
Short differential bobbin pair
Short field zone resolves short defects; differential connection cancels slow drift
Gradual wall thinning in tubing
Long absolute bobbin coil
Long field zone and absolute connection keep slow trends visible
Axial seams in bar and tube at line speed
Encircling (OD) coil, differential
Circumferential currents cut across axial flaws as product feeds through
Second- and third-layer cracks in lap joints
Large low-frequency driver with Hall (or pickup) detection
Large coil pushes the field deep; the detector stays sensitive at low frequency
Static or slow leakage fields (MFL, residual magnetism)
Hall element
Reads flux density itself, down to DC, independent of scan speed

sections.heading: Hall Elements: Reading B Instead of dΦ/dt
sections.body:
Level I introduced the physics; Level II selects with it. A Hall element is a thin semiconductor chip — InSb, InAs, GaAs — carrying a regulated drive current. A flux density component **perpendicular to its face** pushes the moving carriers sideways, and the chip develops a transverse voltage **Vₕ = I·B/(n·q·t)** — directly proportional to **B itself**, polarity included. Nothing has to change for it to respond.

A sensing coil obeys Faraday instead: V = −N·dΦ/dt. Same field, different question — the coil reports the **rate of change**, so its output is proportional to frequency, collapses as frequency falls, and is exactly zero at DC. For detectors of comparable size the two characteristics cross at a **crossover frequency**: below it, the flat Hall response delivers the better signal-to-noise ratio; above it, the rising coil output wins — which is why conventional surface probes from roughly 100 kHz into the megahertz range remain coils.

Three Level II selection points:

- **Size without penalty.** Shrinking a coil strips away the turns and area that make its signal; a Hall element keeps full sensitivity in a sub-millimeter active area.
- **Directionality.** The element reads only the field component perpendicular to its face — orient it to the component carrying the flaw information, or exploit the selectivity to map fields. Gaussmeters and teslameters are Hall instruments.
- **It replaces the detector, not the physics.** Excitation must still be an alternating or moving field, and depth of penetration is still set by frequency, conductivity, and permeability. The instrument now phase-analyzes a sensor voltage rather than a coil impedance, but the display logic — lift-off direction, phase rotation with depth — carries over.

The honest costs: millivolt output that needs amplification, temperature drift that needs compensation, a regulated supply, and a die less rugged than wound copper. Below a few kilohertz — multilayer aerospace structure, MFL, static field verification — those costs buy a detector no coil can match.

sections.caption: Detector selection: coil output rises with frequency while the Hall element stays flat from DC to about 100 kHz. Below the crossover, Hall detection wins — and the excitation field still does the penetrating.

figures.title: Coil diameter sets the field footprint
Shape decisions: shields, length, and current orientation
Choosing the detector: coil vs Hall element

interactive.title: Geometry on the Line: Four Probe Calls
interactive.intro: Four jobs, four selections. For each, pick the coil geometry — or detector — whose field behavior matches the impedance-response requirement. Reason from footprint, fraction-of-field, and frequency response before you click.

steps.prompt: Fatigue cracks start at fastener heads in an aluminum wing skin. You must scan tight against each steel fastener without the geometry signal burying the crack response. Which probe?
options: A 25 mm air-core conductivity probe — its big field guarantees coverage
A long unshielded pancake coil for maximum raw sensitivity
A small-diameter shielded probe with a ferrite core
Any probe will do — shielding only matters on ferromagnetic parts
steps.feedback: The ferrite shield confines the footprint to roughly the winding OD, so the fastener stays outside the field until you are nearly on it — and the small, concentrated field makes a short crack interrupt a large fraction of the eddy current flow. Unshielded or oversized coils ring on the fastener long before the crack speaks.

steps.prompt: You are sorting heat-treated 2024 aluminum parts by conductivity. The machined surfaces are slightly rough. Which probe gives the most stable %IACS readings?
options: A large-diameter air-core absolute probe
The smallest shielded probe in the kit — highest resolution wins
A short differential bobbin pair
A Hall-element gaussmeter held against the surface
steps.feedback: Conductivity measurement wants averaging, not resolution: a broad footprint integrates roughness and local microstructure into one stable bulk reading. A tiny probe turns every tool mark into noise, bobbin coils are for tubes and bores, and a gaussmeter reads static flux density — not conductivity.

steps.prompt: In heat-exchanger tubing you must flag short, isolated ID pits while ignoring slow, gradual variation along the tube. Which bobbin configuration?
options: A long absolute coil
A short differential pair
A single differential coil wound over the probe's full length
An encircling OD coil — inspect the bundle from outside
steps.feedback: A short field zone matches the short defect — the pit fills a large fraction of it — and the differential connection nulls slow trends so the pit stands alone. The long absolute coil answers the opposite question: gradual wall thinning. And in an installed bundle the OD is unreachable — ID bobbin probes exist for exactly that reason.

steps.prompt: A procedure calls for detecting third-layer cracks in a lap joint at 200 Hz using a large driver coil. Which detector completes the probe?
options: A small high-Q absolute coil — the highest-sensitivity winding available
Raise the frequency to 100 kHz so a coil detector can hear the flaw
Either one — detector choice does not affect low-frequency response
A Hall element centered in the driver
steps.feedback: At 200 Hz a sensing coil's dΦ/dt output has collapsed to a sliver, and raising the frequency would shrink the skin depth so the field never reaches the third layer. The Hall element reads B itself at full sensitivity down to DC — the large driver supplies the depth, the Hall chip supplies the hearing.

checkQuestions.question: A probe designer doubles the number of turns on a surface coil while keeping its dimensions and core unchanged. The empty-coil inductance L₀ will:
options: Double
Quadruple — inductance scales with the square of the turns
Stay the same — inductance depends only on the core material
Halve
checkQuestions.explanation: L₀ ∝ N², so twice the turns means four times the inductance. Because X₀ = 2πfL₀ sets the reference for the normalized impedance plane, the operating point your instrument displays moves with it — coil geometry is a test parameter.

checkQuestions.question: Directly beneath the center of a circular surface probe, the eddy current density in the part is:
options: Maximum — the field is strongest on the coil axis
Uniform across the entire footprint
Greatest several diameters outside the winding
Essentially zero, with the peak in a ring beneath the windings
checkQuestions.explanation: The induced currents mirror the circular winding, so they cancel on the centerline and peak under the mean winding radius. That ring-shaped sensitivity is why a small flaw answers loudest as the winding passes over it — and why scan plans specify index and overlap.

checkQuestions.question: A 1 mm crack gives a strong signal under a 3 mm probe but vanishes under a 25 mm sorting coil at the same frequency. The reason is that:
options: Under the small coil, the crack interrupts a much larger fraction of the total eddy current flow
The large coil's field cannot reach the part surface
Small coils always operate at higher frequency
The large coil has greater lift-off
checkQuestions.explanation: Signal amplitude follows the fraction of the field disturbed. The small probe concentrates its field, so the crack blocks a meaningful share of it; under the large coil the same crack disturbs a tiny share of a big averaged field. Frequency and lift-off were unchanged — geometry alone made the difference.

checkQuestions.question: An encircling-coil examination of tubing is LEAST sensitive to which discontinuity?
options: A longitudinal seam on the tube OD
A short axial crack
A thin circumferential crack lying parallel to the induced current flow
A long axial score with varying depth
checkQuestions.explanation: An encircling coil induces currents that flow around the circumference. A flaw must interrupt the current to be seen: axial flaws cut across the flow and respond strongly, while a circumferential crack parallel to it can pass with little or no signal — a limitation noted in encircling-coil practices such as ASTM E243.

checkQuestions.question: For a 250 Hz examination of a three-layer aluminum lap joint, a Hall element is preferred over a sensing coil as the detector because:
options: Hall detection increases the standard depth of penetration
Hall element output is flat down to DC, while a sensing coil's output falls in proportion to frequency
A Hall element eliminates the need for an excitation coil
Hall elements are more temperature-stable than coils
checkQuestions.explanation: Vₕ = I·B/(n·q·t) responds to flux density itself, so the Hall element keeps full sensitivity at 250 Hz where a coil's dΦ/dt output has collapsed. Penetration is still set by frequency and material, excitation is still required, and Hall elements actually need temperature compensation — the advantage is purely in detection.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, ET Level II Topical Outline §2.2.4–2.2.5 (test coil and shape; Hall effect elements)
ASNT SNT-TC-1A (2024), employer-based qualification and certification framework (ET Level II training topic coverage)
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations (electromagnetic testing terminology)
ASTM E243, Standard Practice for Electromagnetic (Eddy Current) Examination of Copper and Copper-Alloy Tubes (encircling-coil practice and orientation limitations)
